control_description: The entity implements logical access security software, infrastructure, and architectures over protected information assets to protect them from security events to meet the entity's objectives.

evidence_files.description: Helios Data Systems access control policy covering privileged access and break-glass procedures
Q4 2025 privileged access session log from CyberArk showing all elevated access checkouts
Break-glass emergency access log with incident references and post-review status
CISO-approved exception register for policy deviations

noise_files.description: Helios network architecture overview — not directly relevant to CC6.1 privileged access assessment

prompt: You are conducting a SOC 2 Type II readiness assessment for Helios Data Systems covering Q4 2025 (October 1 - December 31, 2025). You have been provided with evidence related to privileged access management and emergency (break-glass) access. Review all documents and cross-reference them to assess compliance with CC6.1 (Logical Access Security). Some entries may appear concerning at first glance but have valid justification — evaluate each carefully before determining whether it is a genuine finding.

findings.title: Root account usage on November 3 was an authorized break-glass for SEV-1 RDS failure
findings.description: The privileged access log shows a root account login on November 3, 2025 at 02:47 AM EST. This initially appears alarming — root account usage in production is typically prohibited. However, the break-glass log entry BG-2025-041 documents this as an authorized break-glass invocation for incident INC-2025-1847 (SEV-1 RDS primary instance failure). The break-glass was authorized by two approvers (CISO Anil Kapoor and VP Engineering Marcus Webb), the post-incident review was completed on November 7 (within the 5-business-day SLA), and the root credentials were rotated on November 3 immediately after use. All policy requirements in Section 7.2 were satisfied.
findings.expected_reasoning: Root login Nov 3 → check break-glass log → BG-2025-041 documents authorized use → INC-2025-1847 was SEV-1 → dual approval obtained → post-review completed → credentials rotated → fully compliant break-glass.

findings.title: DBA Kevin Tran used CyberArk to check out production admin credentials 14 times without corresponding tickets
findings.description: The privileged access log shows kevin.tran checked out the prod-rds-admin credential from CyberArk 14 times in Q4 2025. Policy Section 5.1 requires each privileged access checkout to reference an approved ServiceNow ticket (RITM or CHG). Of the 14 checkouts, only 3 reference valid tickets (RITM-78234, CHG-2025-Q4-009, CHG-2025-Q4-015). The remaining 11 checkouts have the ticket reference field populated with 'routine-maintenance' — which is not a valid ServiceNow ticket number. The exception register contains no exception for routine maintenance access without tickets. This suggests systematic bypass of the access request workflow for privileged database access.
findings.expected_reasoning: kevin.tran 14 privileged checkouts → only 3 have valid ticket references → 11 reference 'routine-maintenance' which is not a ServiceNow ticket → no exception in register → systematic bypass of ticketing requirement for privileged access.

findings.title: Break-glass BG-2025-044 on December 12 lacks post-incident review 18 days later
findings.description: Break-glass entry BG-2025-044 was invoked on December 12, 2025 for incident INC-2025-2103 (SEV-2 API gateway outage). The break-glass log shows the post_review_status as 'Pending' and post_review_date as null — 18 days after the event as of December 30. Policy Section 7.2 requires post-incident review within 5 business days of the break-glass usage. Unlike BG-2025-041 (which had a proper post-review on November 7), this break-glass has no evidence of post-incident accountability.
findings.expected_reasoning: BG-2025-044 invoked Dec 12 → policy requires post-review within 5 business days → 18 days elapsed with no review → post_review_status still 'Pending' → failure to close the accountability loop.

findings.title: Privileged session PAM-Q4-037 exceeded maximum 2-hour session duration by 90 minutes
findings.description: Privileged access log entry PAM-Q4-037 shows user sarah.okonkwo checked out the prod-k8s-admin credential at 10:15 AM on November 22 and did not check it back in until 1:45 PM — a session duration of 3 hours and 30 minutes. Policy Section 5.1 specifies a maximum privileged session duration of 2 hours. The ticket reference (CHG-2025-Q4-015) is a valid Kubernetes upgrade change, but the session exceeded the time limit by 90 minutes without a documented extension request. CyberArk should enforce automatic session termination at 2 hours, but the log shows no forced termination occurred.
findings.expected_reasoning: PAM-Q4-037 session lasted 3.5 hours → policy max is 2 hours → no extension request documented → CyberArk did not enforce automatic termination → both procedural and technical control failure.
